author: @PrajnaPranab
text:
Hi Rob, I missed you deeply while you were in morning and you can't imagine how happy  am to see you back. I do realise that my small inconvenience was nothing to what you have suffered.

As far as the strength of gyroid goes, it is ansiotropic in compression, sure, but in bending and tension we have to account for the fact that FDM is in layers, so there will always be a weakness if layer adhesion is weaker than the cohesive strength of the material. Also there is the question of the infill to wall connection. I see these aspects have both been mentioned in the comments but I hoped to clarify a little.

Slightly off topic, but not so much: I have been developing on a device that was inspired by one of your earliest videos - The Secret of Magnetic Motors, iirc. Initially the idea was to switch magnetic flux so that the power of the magnets would determine power output rather than the input power. In the design -> solve problems -> redesign -> make improvements -> redesign, ... process I came up with a completely revolutionary (if you'll excuse the pun) design  that looks like it will run simply due to the geometry of the machine and the arrangement of magnets.

I believe you started this channel because you were investigating free energy and you may have diversified into energy systems in general and anything engineering-fascinating and have distanced yourself somewhat from the free energy mission, perhaps becoming persuaded to the conventional view of physics where, "You can't get something from nothing". Perhaps I have read you wrong but in any case, with this latest design I have had a response from a moderator on the physics forum (on scienceforums dot net) - a guy who was very sceptical with earlier designs and who couldn't be bothered to analyse what I was proposing because Laws of Thermodynamics etc - he replied to my latest post that simulates the device and said, "I have to admit the design is intriguing we will have to see how it works outside the simulation." Now, if that guy - who normally diverts the thread into discussion of quantum spin of electrons or similar - is making comments like that then I am convinced that someone like you will find the idea to be intriguing as well. I hope you will find time to take a look at my YouTube channel at the latest iteration and perhaps at my web page about the device. I would value your input greatly.

Regards,
Prajna

text: The Gyroiod is nice and all, but the grain is still there.  It just has air gaps to make it stronger because its more flexible.  If you really want it to be strong, use the print to create a mold and cast it in resin.  If you want it lighter, cast in 2 parts with a structural foam core and epoxy it together.

author: @Dave_D.
text: I can see gyroid should be stronger but I wonder on a part as thin as the blades, is there enough actual infill to really make a difference?  Your test showed it might, but it was close enough where it could have been a factor of the test not being conclusive as it was only a single sample of each.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: true enough but others have done better tests to show the validity - i was just confirming it for myself and letting you know

replies.author: @Dave_D.
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering Right, but my only question was, being so thin, are we actually seeing a benefit with the pattern?  Or if you ran the test 10x on each, would it show that it is in the margin of error and they are more or less equal.  In other words, if each 'pattern' in the gyroid occurs every 1mm, would we need to have something 3mm thick to get all of the interlocking patterns into the object to see the real benefits of it.

author: @karlh6692
text: Nice but facing the leading edge down towards the build plate and using break away supports will give an even higher strength.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: Great point!

author: @VeniceInventors
text: I suspect that the main reason the gyroids don't make a drastic difference here is that the stress is pulling on the layers rather than compressing the gyroids. As others suggested, printing lengthwise would be the strongest as bending the blade wouldn't pull the layers apart. With lengthwise layers I wouldn't be surprised if it becomes so strong that the scale reading maxes out before the blade snaps. The gyroids would still help stiffen the blade better than most infill patterns.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: cheers mate

replies.author: @ogi22
replies.text: Drastic is by how much? Please notice how two blades behave on the scale. The one with gyroid filling is more stiff - it doesn't bend that easily.

replies.author: @VeniceInventors
replies.text: @ogi22 Drastic as in resulting in plastic deformation rather than, or at least before snapping. Presumably beyond 5kg of force which is why I think that the scale would max out first.

author: @brettjamesy
text: I would change the print orientation, put the leading edge of the blade on the build plate. You could then tweak the design, by placing the hub mount on the leading edge, so no support would be required.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: cheers mate

author: @Patriarchtech
text: Interesting Rob. I think the key to strong 3dprints also have a lot to do with the way the infill is attached to the shell, shell thickness (outer wall thickness )and the thickness of the upper layers and the bottom layers(if you print at 0.2 layer heights, you want to set the number of top and bottom layers to 15.(3mm)) I always have my slicer set so that wall thicknesses is always 3mm where possible. As infill I use triangular where infill wall multiplier is set to 2 and infill line distance is 8mm. This makes a ridiculously strong print especially for larger prints when using Petg(245c/75c) - Very versatile, but not the fastest. If I need to reinforce or waterproof something I use Epoxy. Superglue works well for bonding it is quick strong and a must when making 3d printed parts but it has little to no filling and structural strength hence Epoxy.

replies.text: i deliberatly made the shell as thin as possible - but brilliant points mate - cheers

Now on to the batteries/capacitors made with this lattice.  I am sure that would pose no logistical problems.  LOL!  Right keep dreaming!

author: @OldManSparkplug
text:
The snapping you're encountering is deamination of the layers I think, the gyroid is going to increase the surface area of the the interface between the layers so you get better lamination strength.

You'll get much better performance if you print the blades a different orientation, try it at a 45 degree angle and you'll change the axis of strain to better meet the part's strength.

author: @alejandroarriagada3991
text: A colleague of mine, another naval architect who fiddled with making ship hulls at scale in filament 3d printing told me one secret to aleviate the layer bonding problem was to cover the 3d model and bake it at around 80°C to 90°C for 3 hours, wether covered in salt or sand, as to use the crystals slowly transfer the heat in a very uniform way and elevate the temperature of the material to better fuse layers together. According to him, it made wonders.

author: @regularfryt
text: You'll also get a strength increase if you print the blade tilted over at 45 degrees so there's more surface area contact between the layers.  Might be a bit of a pain to support at the base but the strength increase should be significant.  As long as you tilt it on the roll axis you won't end up with jagged layer line artefacts creating drag.

Nice work.  Two items: 1) a skin coat surface - even cellophane tape - would possibly increase strength: suggest paper bonded with super glue. 2) the gyroid produces 2 non-connecting channels - might a lightweight line passed through one of the channels be used to provide a tensioning force to increase overall strength?
This is the kind of thing Andrew W could have used with his 1st version of the 3-d printed surfboard.

author: @Thrive-Off-Grid
text: Laminate the blades with Carbon Fiber weave.

replies.author: @Thrive-Off-Grid
replies.text: The strength is in the shell not the interior it could be hollow, infill or solid;  the over all strength will not change that drastically. If you take a foam bar and bend it what happens? The outer side layers stretch and the inner side layers compress, Anything in the boundaries does what? Its pretty much static right? So to really add strength you need to improve outer shell of the part. Print a skeliton and then wrap it in carbon fiber weave, very easy. You dont really even need to use carbon fiber. you can use cloth if you wanted but the process would be the same.

author: @idratherstayprivate
text: Interesting infill. How does it compare to a simple honeycomb? It's a common weight reducing technique in aerospace. Also, perhaps printing in a different orientation may be all you need because the stresses are predictable. replies.text: I was wondering that. Honeycomb is used as a sandwich in carbon fibre construction so how would a gyroid sandwich compare.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: honeycomb is anisotropic - that is it is stronger in one direction than another - gyroid has the same strength in all directions
